   For decades,  politicians have talked about the U.S. achieving energy independence, a seemingly elusive goal of producing enough fuels to avoid relying on the rest of the world to fill up gas tanks and keep electricity flowing.
 The intrigue: It's elusive no more. The U.S. produced more petroleum than it consumed in 2020, and the numbers were essentially in balance in 2021, according to the Energy Information Administration.
 Why it matters: The surge in oil prices taking place in 2022 has radically different implications for the U.S. economy — and for key geopolitical relationships in the Middle East and Russia — than in past episodes when energy prices have risen.
 The big picture: In the past, when oil prices spiked, the impact on the U.S. economy was straightforward: It made America poorer, as more of our income went overseas to pay for imported energy.
 Now, after the shale gas revolution of the last 15 years, the impact is more subtle. Higher fuel prices disadvantage consumers and energy-intensive industries, yes. But there is a counteracting surge in incomes for domestic energy producers and their workers.
 Higher oil prices no longer depress overall measures of prosperity like GDP and national income, but rather shift it around toward certain regions. Texas and North Dakota win; Massachusetts and North Carolina lose.
 By the numbers: As recently as 2010, America imported 9.4 million barrels a day of oil more than it exported. That had swung to a 650,000 barrel per day surplus in 2020, and preliminary numbers for 2021 show trade pretty much in balance last year.
 Worth noting: To the degree the U.S. does still import oil, more of it is coming from our closest ally. Canada was the source of 51% of U.S. petroleum imports in the first 10 months of 2021, compared with 8% from the Persian Gulf.
 By contrast, the Gulf states supplied more than 30% of American petroleum imports in 2008.
 Free idea for an international relations professor: Assign an essay question on how the changing economics of energy could affect America's stance in Middle Eastern diplomacy.
 The impact on geopolitics extends to a potential Russian invasion of Ukraine. Disruptions to European energy supplies would have a less direct effect on the U.S. than they might have in an earlier era.
 The bottom line: When oil prices spike, it's no longer a problem for overall growth—but because energy is a global market, it still means pain for American consumers and the sitting administration.
title: 美国现在能源独立
title_en: The U.S. is now energy independent

想读一个疯狂的否认终身教职的故事吗？2018年，我被拒绝在麻省理工学院任职。我是系里唯一的西班牙裔教师，有超过13k的引文，2本书；发表在《自然》、《科学》杂志上的论文；帕纳斯。从来没有人给我一个理由&amp；我收到的唯一一封信告诉我要查看网站/1.🧵
在否认之后，我要求会见我的部门主管。他没有提供任何理由，只是“决定来自于上面”；他或部门无能为力。所以几个月后，我要求再次与他会面；要求与总统会面。
他后退了一步；他告诉我，这不是“真正的高于”，而是学校的决定。然后他劝阻我去见总统，尽管我后来还是设法去见了他。但由于学校的理论也没有道理，我要求几个月后再召开一次会议。
我试图理解。为了得到一个原因而不是查看网站。最终，总统会告诉我一个真正的原因，但在那之前，我能总结出的是，该部门对我和；提出了一个薄弱的案例，以便学校可以将其击落。
我知道我不会从我的部门主管那里得到一个直接的答复，所以我写了一个上诉案件，并要求与总统会面。如果我要上诉，我想以良好的方式上诉。最终，总统给了我一个永远改变了我的答案。
我记得走进他的办公室；看到查尔斯河的景色。总统知道我是谁（因为我的研究），并问我为什么在那里。他告诉我，他从来没有听说过我的终身教职案，因为他从来没有听说过。
我们用西班牙语进行了交谈，我认为这提供了一些坦诚，否则就不会有这种坦诚。我向他提交了我的上诉文件；问他上诉的事，因为我想让他友好地上诉。
他告诉我，除非是正式提交，否则他不想碰这个案子，但他可以给我一些建议。所以我问他是否对我的情况有什么建议。他说：
“听着，塞萨尔，事实是，如果你是一个平庸的研究员，但你的部门想留住你，他们会找到办法留住你。如果你是一个强大的研究员，你的部门想摆脱你，他们会找到办法摆脱你。”
我终于得到了“答案”麻省理工学院院长告诉我，终身教职与研究、生产力或功绩无关。这是关于办公室政治的；被你的部门喜欢。他不是恶意的。我觉得他很善良；诚实的所以我问他我是否应该上诉。
他的下一个答案更具战略性。他告诉我，他当然不能劝我不要上诉，但我应该考虑以下几点：
其他大学可能会考虑我，可能会打电话给我的系主任，要求推荐我。我的部门负责人要做的唯一一件事就是扼杀我的机会，就是回答说他正忙着提供建议。所以我应该小心别让他生气。
我没有上诉。那一年改变了我。在那之前，我相信精英统治是不完美的。毕竟，科学工作是可复制和可验证的。它的采用在引文中也有不完善的表述。是时候长大了；接受愤世嫉俗。
事后看来，这是显而易见的。但我选择学术界的原因之一是因为我相信思想的力量；努力工作。
我记得我被剥夺任期的那一天。我不得不接待一位即将退休的教授。我原计划下午三点见他，但在中午被告知我拒绝了。所以我只好忍气吞声，欢迎他来我的办公室。但我不能假装什么都没发生，所以我告诉他：
“很抱歉，我不能简单地谈论研究。我刚刚得知我的终身职位申请被驳回了。”他看着我&amp；他立刻说：“但是如果你的案子被批准了，高级教师中谁会赢呢？”他理解这个游戏。
*我还有更多要说。我可能会把我在“研究所”经历的心理伤害写进一本书但正如你所理解的，这些都是很难谈论的事情。它们是生活在我内心的风暴&amp；晚上叫醒我。我花了很多年的时间来分享这个。
note_en:
Want to read an insane tenure denial story? I was denied tenure at MIT in 2018. I was the only Hispanic faculty at my department, had 13k+ citations, 2 books, & papers in Nature, Science, & PNAS. I was never given a reason & the only letter I got told me to check the website./1🧵
 After the denial I asked to meet my department head. He did not provide any reason other than “the decision came from above” & that there was nothing him or the department could do. So a few months later I asked to meet with him again & requested a meeting with the president.
 He backpedaled & told me that it was not “really above,” but a decision from the school. He then dissuaded me to meet the president, although I did manage to meet him later. But since the school theory didn’t make sense either, I asked for another meeting a few months later.
 I was trying to understand. To get a reason other than check the website. Eventually the president would tell me a real reason, but what I could put together up until that time was that the department was divided about me & presented a weak case so the school could shoot it down.
 I understood I was not going to get a straight answer from my department head, so I wrote an appeal case and asked for a meeting with the president. If I was going to appeal, I wanted to do it in good terms.   Eventually, the president gave me an answer that changed me forever.
 I remember entering his office & seeing the view of the Charles river. The president knew who I was (because of my research) and asked me why I was there. He told me he had never heard about my tenure case since it had never reached him.
 We had our conversation in Spanish, and I think that afforded some candor that would not have been there otherwise. I presented him my appeal papers & asked him about appealing, because I wanted it to do it amicably.
 He told me he didn’t want to touch the case unless it was an official submission, but that he could provide me with some advice. So I asked him if he had any advice about my situation. He said:
 “Look Cesar, the truth is that, if you are a mediocre researcher, but your department wants to keep you, they’ll find a way to keep you. And if you are a strong researcher, and your department wants to get rid of you, they’ll find a way to get rid of you.”
 I finally got “an answer.” The president of MIT told me that tenure was not about research, productivity, or merit. It was about office politics & being liked by your department. He didn’t mean it in a bad way. I think he was being kind & honest. So I ask him if I should appeal.
 His next answer was more strategic. He told me that of course he could not advice me not to appeal, but that I should consider the following:
 Other universities might be considering me, and would probably call my department head asking for a recommendation. The only thing my dept. head had to do to kill my chances was to reply that he was to busy to provide a recommendation. So I should be careful to not upset him.
 I did not appeal.  That year changed me. Before then I believed in an imperfect meritocracy. After all, scientific work is reproducible and verifiable. Its adoption is also expressed imperfectly in citations.  It was time to grow up & accept the cynicism.
 In hindsight it was obvious. But one of the reasons why I choose academia is because I believed in the power of ideas & hardwork.
 I remember the day of my tenure denial. I had to host a professor who was near retirement. I was scheduled to meet him at 3pm but was informed about my denial at noon. So I had to suck it up and welcome him in my office. But I couldn’t act like nothing happened, so I told him:
 “I am sorry, I am not going to be able to simply talk about research. I just learned that my tenure case was denied.” He looked at me & said instantly: “But if your case had been approved, who in the senior faculty would have won?”  He understood the game.
  *I have more to tell. I could probably fill a book with the psychological damage I experienced at “the institute.” But as you may understand these are difficult things to talk about. They are the storm that lives within me & wakes me up at night. It took me years to share this.
title: “麻省理工学院院长告诉我，终身教职与研究或学术成就无关。”
title_en: “The president of MIT told me that tenure was not about research or merit.”
